**THE INTERVIEW - A1 LEVEL (5 minutes)**
**Target:** 8-12 words per sentence, max 12 words
**Connectors:** "and", "but", "when"
**Word count target:** ~400-450 words

---

Carlos sat at his computer and looked at his email. He received another rejection email, and this was the 46th rejection. He felt sad and hopeless, but he had one more interview tomorrow. This was his last chance to find a job, and he needed this job very much.

Carlos came to America six months ago, and he could not speak English well. He studied English every day, but he still struggled with conversations. He applied to many jobs, and he got many rejections. People said his English was not good enough, and this made him feel bad.

The next morning, Carlos woke up early and felt nervous. He practiced his answers all night, and he tried to remember important words. He put on his best suit, and he looked in the mirror. He told himself that he could do this, but he still felt scared.

Carlos arrived at the office building, and he walked inside. The building was very tall and beautiful, and many people walked around him. He found the elevator, and he pressed the button for floor 15. His heart beat fast, and he felt his hands shake.

Carlos entered the interview room, and he saw three people sitting at a table. They smiled at him, and they asked him to sit down. The first question was easy, and Carlos answered it well. But then they asked him to tell them about himself, and Carlos forgot everything he practiced.

Carlos felt panic inside his chest, and his mind went completely blank. He could not remember any English words, and he could not speak. The interviewers waited quietly, and Carlos felt embarrassed. He wanted to run away, but he knew he could not do that.

Carlos closed his eyes for a moment, and he took a deep breath. He thought about why he came to America, and he remembered his dream. He thought about his family back home, and he remembered their support. He opened his eyes, and he decided to speak from his heart.

Carlos started speaking slowly, and he told them about his journey. He talked about coming to America with hope, and he talked about learning English every day. He talked about his determination to succeed, and he talked about never giving up. The words came naturally, and he felt more confident.

The interviewers listened carefully, and they nodded their heads. They asked him more questions, and Carlos answered them honestly. He did not use perfect English, but he communicated clearly. He showed them his passion and his hard work, and they could see his determination.

At the end of the interview, Carlos shook their hands, and he thanked them. He walked out of the building, and he felt proud of himself. He did not know if he would get the job, but he knew he did his best. He spoke from his heart, and that was enough.

Two days later, Carlos received an email, and he opened it nervously. The email said congratulations, and Carlos got the job. He felt happy and excited, and he called his family immediately. He proved that determination matters more than perfect English, and he achieved his dream.
